content.assessment: This proposal most directly mirrors the Polish structural mechanism: delaying first tracking by converting an early-selection point into a unified comprehensive lower-secondary phase, precisely analogous to Poland's 1999 gimnazjum reform. Polish evidence [moderate] shows this mechanism correlated with PISA gains and documented reductions in socio-economic performance gaps between 2000 and 2015, particularly benefiting formerly vocational-track students, lending credible if not conclusive support to the core structural logic. However, the lens equally surfaces the sharpest cautionary evidence in the set: Poland's 2017 reversal, executed within roughly 10 months rather than through phased transition, produced acute distributional harm — 3,173 unplaced Warsaw secondary students, ~7,000 school closures, and threatened job losses for 10,000 teachers [strong]. The proposal explicitly designs around this by mandating multi-cohort phasing, which is the correct implementation-sequence lesson to draw from Poland's failure mode. A further reversibility caution: Poland's 2018 PISA results attributed to the old system rather than the new one illustrate how attribution of short-term post-reform outcomes to reform effectiveness is unreliable, and any evaluation of this proposal's early results should apply the same caution. The proposal also confronts the fact that Polish-style structural delay alone is not self-sufficient — bundling with anti-sorting rules is required, and the proposal does bundle this, aligning with the identified limitation that pure age-of-tracking reform has limited effect without addressing other selection junctures.
opportunities: A phased, multi-year implementation model could avoid the reversal risk that erased Poland's structural gains after nearly two decades, potentially yielding more durable distributional effects
strengths: Directly replicates Poland's 1999 structural mechanism (comprehensive lower-secondary delay) that correlated with measured PISA gains and reduced socio-economic performance gaps, particularly for previously disadvantaged track students (F-live-polish_reform-03)
Explicitly designs implementation as multi-cohort phased transition, directly responding to the documented failure mode of Poland's abrupt 2017 reversal (F-live-polish_reform-05)
Recognizes that structural delay alone is insufficient and bundles it with anti-sorting rules addressing the independent primary-choice segregation mechanism (F-live-polish_reform-07)
threats: Reversibility remains a live threat: Poland's reform, despite documented gains over 2000-2015, was fully reversed in 2017, showing that structural mechanisms of this kind are politically contestable and not self-sustaining absent durable political consensus (F-live-polish_reform-04)
weaknesses: Even well-intentioned phasing carries residual risk given Poland's demonstrated case that structural transitions of this scale are prone to capacity and staffing disruption regardless of intent (F-live-polish_reform-05)

Short-term outcome measurement after implementation is unreliable as a signal of reform effectiveness, since Poland's 2018 PISA results were attributable to the outgoing cohort under the old system, not the new one — a similar attribution trap could mislead early evaluation of this proposal (F-live-polish_reform-06)
